---
title: "Day 27 — SLP3 Ch7 - the same model in prose"
subtitle: "Wednesday, 19 August 2026 · Week 4 · Build a GPT · Study day · 3-4 h"
---

::: {.day-nav .kind-study}
[← Day 26](day-26.qmd) · [All 50 days](../timeline.qmd) · [Day 28 →](day-28.qmd)
:::

::: {.why}
Reading the textbook chapter after writing the code inverts the usual order, which makes it much faster, because you already know what most of the terms point at in a file you wrote.
:::

## What you should be able to do tonight

- You can name each decoding strategy in Ch7, say what it does to the probability distribution before a token is drawn, and identify from your Day 25 table which one produced the most readable Shakespeare.
- You can convert your model's val loss into perplexity and into bits per character, and explain why that number is not comparable to a BPE-level model's perplexity.
- You can point at the line of `gpt_mine.py` that implements the training objective Ch7 writes as an equation.

## Tasks <span class='task-total'>210 min</span>

::: {.task-list}
::: {.task}
<input type="checkbox" class="task-check"> [60 min]{.task-min} Read SLP3 Ch7 through the end of the decoding material with `gpt_mine.py` open, and locate each named thing in your code: the unembedding layer is your `nn.Linear(n_embd, vocab_size)`, teacher forcing is why you never feed the model its own output during training, the context window is `block_size`, autoregressive conditioning is the loop in `generate`.
:::
::: {.task}
<input type="checkbox" class="task-check"> [45 min]{.task-min} Implement top-p (nucleus) sampling in `GPTLanguageModel.generate`: sort logits descending, take the cumulative softmax, keep the smallest prefix whose mass exceeds p, mask the rest to -inf, renormalize. Run p over {0.9, 0.95, 1.0} on your best checkpoint and add the rows to the Day 25 decoding table.
:::
::: {.task}
<input type="checkbox" class="task-check"> [45 min]{.task-min} Convert your val loss into the numbers Ch7's evaluation section uses. Your loss is cross-entropy in nats per character, so a loss of 1.48 is exp(1.48) = 4.4 in perplexity and 1.48/ln(2) = 2.13 bits per character. Write in `slp3_ch7_notes.md` why a BPE-level model reporting perplexity 20 is not therefore worse, and what you would have to normalize by to compare them.
:::
::: {.task}
<input type="checkbox" class="task-check"> [30 min]{.task-min} Read the pretraining-data section - what is actually in Common Crawl, what filtering and deduplication get applied before training. Write two sentences on what that implies for the PubMed abstract corpus you trained BPE on in Week 3 and will reuse from Day 33: what your filtering step would be, and what field you would deduplicate on.
:::
::: {.task}
<input type="checkbox" class="task-check"> [30 min]{.task-min} Write the paragraph that joins the two halves of this week: which parts of Ch7 you had already implemented before you read them, and which parts the textbook has that your code does not - top-p until an hour ago, the KV cache, beam search, anything about the data.
:::
:::

## Resources

- [SLP3 Ch7 - Large Language Models](https://web.stanford.edu/~jurafsky/slp3/7.pdf) [textbook]{.res-kind}
- [SLP3 Ch3 - N-gram Language Models (perplexity, for the comparison)](https://web.stanford.edu/~jurafsky/slp3/3.pdf) [textbook]{.res-kind}
- [SLP3 landing page](https://web.stanford.edu/~jurafsky/slp3/) [textbook]{.res-kind}

## Deliverable

::: {.deliverable}
Top-p implemented and benchmarked as new rows in the Day 25 decoding table, plus `slp3_ch7_notes.md` containing the perplexity conversion worked out in nats and bits, the comparability argument, and a list of Ch7 topics your code does not implement.
:::

## Checkpoint

::: {.checkpoint}
Beam search reliably finds higher-probability sequences than sampling does. Explain why that is a defect rather than a feature for open-ended generation, and what property of human text it conflicts with.
:::

## Prepare for tomorrow

Ten minutes tonight so Day 28, Thursday, 20 August 2026 starts clean. This is what the evening email sends you.

::: {.task-list .prep}
::: {.task}
<input type="checkbox" class="task-check"> Collect `gpt_mine.py`, `experiments.md`, `samples.md`, `nanogpt_diff.md`, `slp3_ch7_notes.md` and `stuck.md` into one folder. Tomorrow you are assembling, not hunting.
:::
::: {.task}
<input type="checkbox" class="task-check"> Run `sum(p.numel() for p in model.parameters())` on your best config and write the single number down somewhere you will not look at until tomorrow afternoon.
:::
::: {.task}
<input type="checkbox" class="task-check"> Pick the three generations you will actually show: one good, one that fails in an interesting way, one bigram sample for contrast.
:::
:::
